worth_it_explanation: Private marketplace (PMP) deals and programmatic guaranteed (PG) deals represent negotiated inventory access at preferred terms. When a deal is registered in the Yahoo DSP Registered Deals Dashboard but not properly targeted on a Line, or when the Line's bid, budget, or targeting is too restrictive to win impressions, the negotiated inventory goes to other buyers while the account continues purchasing from the open exchange at higher cost. These checks surface quickly in the Exchanges and Deals targeting section of each Line.
intro: Deals in Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) let an advertiser access publisher inventory at negotiated terms outside the open exchange. A private marketplace deal grants first-look access to a publisher's inventory at a floor price the seller sets. A programmatic guaranteed deal commits both the buyer and the seller to a fixed impression volume at a fixed CPM. For either deal type to deliver, the deal must be registered in the Registered Deals Dashboard, the deal must be explicitly targeted on a Line in the Exchanges and Deals section, and that Line must be active, within its flight dates, and have sufficient budget and bid headroom to win against the floor. Yahoo DSP enforces a mutual-exclusivity rule: a Line must target either exchanges or deals, not both. These checks confirm that negotiated deals are delivering as expected and that the account is not carrying stale or unreachable deals that block open-exchange buying on the same Lines.

audit_checks.title: Registered PMP or PG deals are not delivering on targeted Lines
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A PG deal carries a delivery commitment on both sides. When it does not deliver, the advertiser loses the guaranteed inventory while the publisher may still invoice against the contracted volume. A PMP deal that does not spend means first-look inventory at the negotiated floor goes to other buyers, and the account pays open-market rates for equivalent impressions.

educate.base:
In Yahoo DSP, registering a deal in the Registered Deals Dashboard makes the deal available to target, but it does not cause any Line to bid on it. A deal must be explicitly added to a Line's Exchanges and Deals targeting section before any impressions can run. Until that targeting step is completed, the deal sits registered but unspent regardless of its status in the dashboard.

Even after a deal is targeted on a Line, three configuration gaps can cause it to deliver nothing:

The Line's bid is below the deal's floor price. Yahoo DSP enforces the deal floor at auction time. A Line bidding below the floor price will not win impressions from the deal even when the deal is the only inventory source the Line targets.

The Line's daily or total budget is exhausted before the deal's available inventory is reached. Budget pacing at the campaign level can also pull spend away from the Line before the deal has a chance to serve.

The Line's audience or placement targeting is so narrow that it excludes most of the publisher's traffic the deal was meant to capture. A deal negotiated for a publisher's run-of-site inventory delivers nothing if the Line targets only a single section of that site that does not generate enough eligible impressions.

A few signs point to a registered deal that is not serving:
  • The Registered Deals Dashboard shows the deal with no impressions or spend over its flight window
  • The deal is not listed in the Exchanges and Deals section of any active Line in the campaign
  • The Line targeting the deal shows spend from open-exchange inventory but zero impressions attributed to the deal
You may want to confirm the deal's floor price against the Line's bid before escalating to the publisher. A bid-floor gap is the most common recoverable cause of a deal not spending.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, PG deals are most often negotiated around seasonal peaks such as holiday shopping periods or product launch windows. You may want to confirm the deal's start and end dates align with the Line's flight dates and that the Line's bid is above the floor the publisher set for that seasonal inventory.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, PMP deals on contextually relevant B2B or trade-media publishers provide access to qualified professional audiences outside the open exchange. You may want to confirm the Line's audience targeting is not so narrowly scoped that it disqualifies most of the publisher's traffic the deal was intended to reach.

learn_more.source_title: Yahoo Buying Guide: Targeting Microsoft Monetize inventory in Yahoo DSP

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP's Registered Deals Dashboard documentation, deal floor pricing guidance, and deal delivery reporting are available in the Yahoo DSP Help Center, which requires a platform login to access.

investigate.intro: Confirm each registered deal shows impressions over its flight period, is targeted on at least one active Line, and that the Line's bid is above the deal's floor price.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Navigate to 'Inventory' in the left pane and open 'Registered Deals Dashboard'. Review impressions and spend for each deal over the relevant date range. Note any deal showing zero impressions during its intended flight window.

For any deal with no impressions, open the deal detail and note its floor price. Then navigate to the campaign Lines. Open each Line and go to its Targeting settings. In the 'Exchanges and Deals' section, confirm whether this deal ID appears in the Line's targeting criteria.

If the deal is not targeted on any Line, that is the immediate root cause. If it is targeted, check the Line's bid against the deal's floor price. A Line bidding below the floor will not win any deal impressions.

Confirm the Line is active, within its flight dates, and has remaining budget at both the Line and campaign level. A paused or budget-exhausted Line cannot win deal inventory even if the targeting and bid are correct.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per registered deal, whether it shows impressions over its flight period, whether it is targeted on at least one active Line, and whether the Line's bid is at or above the deal's floor price. A deal with spend and a correctly configured active Line passes. A deal with zero spend is a fail, recorded with the deal name, its floor price, the bid on any assigned Line (or a note that no Line targets it), and the Line's budget status.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's name, floor price, impression count, the Lines targeting it, and those Lines' bids and budget status to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more registered PMP or PG deals on {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP account are not delivering impressions during their intended flight window. In at least one case, the deal is registered in the Registered Deals Dashboard but not targeted on any active Line, so the negotiated inventory is available but no campaign is eligible to win it. Where a deal is targeted, the Line's bid is below the deal's floor price, preventing any impressions from winning. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each non-spending deal, confirming it is targeted in the Exchanges and Deals section of the correct Line, setting the Line's bid at or above the deal's floor price, and verifying the Line is active with sufficient budget to serve.

audit_checks.title: Lines targeting deals also have broad open-exchange settings that bypass the negotiated inventory
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Yahoo DSP enforces a mutual-exclusivity rule: a Line must target either exchanges or deals, not both. When this constraint leads practitioners to create a separate open-exchange Line running alongside a deal Line under the same campaign, the two Lines can compete for budget and audience. The open-exchange Line may exhaust campaign budget before the deal Line has had a chance to serve the negotiated inventory, defeating the purpose of the deal.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) requires that a Line's targeting in the Exchanges and Deals section choose one mode: either target specific exchanges (open marketplace buying) or target specific deal IDs (negotiated inventory). A Line cannot do both simultaneously.

To reach both, a campaign can run parallel Lines. One Line targets the deal, and a companion Line targets open-exchange exchanges for the same audience and placement goal. The parallel structure is valid, but it creates a budget-competition risk that can prevent deal Lines from ever winning meaningful volume.

Three patterns indicate the parallel structure is working against the deal:

The campaign's budget pacing algorithm allocates spend proportionally to whichever Line bids most aggressively. If the open-exchange Line uses a higher bid or a more flexible audience, it can capture most of the campaign's daily budget before the deal Line has served its intended volume.

The deal Line and the open-exchange Line target overlapping audiences. When a user is eligible for both, the campaign runs an internal priority contest, and the open-exchange Line may win because it can access a wider pool of available impressions faster.

The deal Line has a more conservative bid than the open-exchange Line, leaving the deal Line underspending while the open-exchange Line runs at full pace.

A few signs point to this pattern:
  • A campaign contains both a deal-targeting Line and an exchange-targeting Line with the same audience and placement parameters
  • The deal Line shows very low impression volume relative to the exchange Line over the same period
  • The deal Line's budget shows consistent underspend even though the deal has available inventory
You may want to review the bid strategy and budget allocation between the two Lines before restructuring them. In some cases, adjusting the deal Line's bid or giving it priority budget allocation resolves the competition without requiring a campaign rebuild.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser running both deal-targeted and open-exchange Lines in the same campaign, you may want to confirm that the deal Line carries the higher bid if the negotiated inventory represents higher-value publisher placements. Letting the open-exchange Line bid higher can cause the campaign to fulfill its goals through open-market inventory instead of the deal.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a deal with a specialized B2B publisher is most valuable when the deal Line is positioned to win that publisher's inventory before the open-exchange Line exhausts the campaign's budget on lower-quality placements. You may want to set deal Lines to a dedicated budget cap rather than sharing a campaign-level budget with open-exchange Lines.

learn_more.source_title: Yahoo Buying Guide: Targeting exchanges and deals in Yahoo DSP

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP's documentation on Line-level budget allocation, bid strategy interactions between parallel Lines, and deal prioritization within a campaign is available in the Yahoo DSP Help Center, which requires a platform login to access.

investigate.intro: For each campaign with deal-targeting Lines, confirm whether companion open-exchange Lines exist, and if so, whether the budget allocation and bid strategy allow the deal Lines to win their intended volume.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Open each active campaign and review the Lines list. Note which Lines have 'Deals' selected in their Exchanges and Deals targeting section and which have 'Exchanges' selected.

For any campaign with both deal-targeting Lines and exchange-targeting Lines, compare their audience and placement targeting parameters. Record whether the two Line types target overlapping audiences or placements.

Compare the impression volume and spend for the deal-targeting Lines versus the exchange-targeting Lines over the same date range. Note whether the deal Lines are consistently underspending relative to the exchange Lines.

Check whether the campaign uses a shared budget that both Line types draw from, or whether each Line has its own budget cap. A shared campaign budget with no Line-level caps allows the faster-spending Line type to exhaust the budget.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, whether both deal-targeting Lines and exchange-targeting Lines are present. For campaigns with both types, the practitioner notes whether the deal Lines show substantially lower spend than the exchange Lines over the same period, whether a shared campaign budget could allow the exchange Lines to exhaust the budget first, and whether the audience and placement parameters overlap. A campaign where deal Lines show proportionate spend relative to their intended volume passes. A campaign where deal Lines consistently underspend while exchange Lines run at full pace, with overlapping audience and shared budget, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the relative spend of each Line type.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each campaign's deal-targeting Line names and their spend alongside any companion exchange-targeting Lines over the same period to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that campaigns on {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP account contain both deal-targeting Lines and open-exchange Lines sharing a campaign-level budget, with overlapping audience parameters. Because Yahoo DSP requires a Line to target either exchanges or deals exclusively, the two Line types run in parallel within the same campaign. The open-exchange Lines are outpacing the deal Lines in spend, which means the negotiated inventory is going largely unused while the campaign fulfills its impression goals through open-market buying. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the budget allocation between deal and exchange Lines, setting Line-level budget caps to guarantee deal Lines receive sufficient budget to fulfill their intended volume, and adjusting bid strategy so the deal Lines are positioned to win their negotiated inventory before the campaign budget is consumed by open-exchange spending.

audit_checks.title: Inactive or expired deals remain targeted on active Lines, creating no-bid noise
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A Line that targets an expired or seller-deactivated deal sends bid requests into inventory the seller is no longer offering. The Line wastes auction participation overhead on inventory it cannot win, and the stale deal ID can obscure reporting by appearing in targeting configurations without generating any attributable impressions.

educate.base:
Deals in Yahoo DSP have a lifecycle tied to the seller's willingness to honor the negotiated terms. A deal that was active during a prior campaign flight may expire when the flight ends, or the seller may deactivate it when inventory conditions change. Yahoo DSP will continue to show the deal ID in a Line's targeting configuration after the deal expires or is deactivated unless the practitioner manually removes it.

A Line with a stale deal ID in its targeting is not broken in any visible way. The Line remains active and continues to bid. However, every bid request it sends against the expired deal ID returns no available inventory. The seller is no longer offering impressions under that deal. The Line either shows zero delivery from the deal or the deal ID disappears from impression-level reporting while still appearing in the targeting configuration.

Stale deals accumulate when campaigns are copied or cloned from prior flights without reviewing the Exchanges and Deals targeting. A deal that was valid for a Q4 campaign may be copied into a Q1 campaign where the same deal no longer exists or has different terms.

A few signs point to stale deals in a Line's targeting:
  • A deal ID appears in a Line's Exchanges and Deals targeting but shows no impressions in the Registered Deals Dashboard over the current flight period
  • The deal in the Registered Deals Dashboard shows a status other than active, such as expired or inactive
  • The Line was created by copying a prior campaign's Line without updating the targeting
You may want to cross-reference the deal status in the Registered Deals Dashboard against each Line's targeting configuration as part of any campaign copy review. Removing stale deal IDs from targeting does not require rebuilding the Line.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser that runs separate campaign flights for each seasonal period, stale deals from a prior flight are most likely to appear in Lines created by copying the prior campaign. You may want to establish a pre-launch checklist that includes reviewing the Exchanges and Deals targeting on every copied Line before activating the new flight.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running ongoing always-on campaigns, deals negotiated with specific publishers may expire when the insertion order (IO) with the publisher ends. You may want to confirm deal status at the start of each quarter rather than assuming deals negotiated during onboarding remain active indefinitely.

learn_more.source_title: Yahoo Buying Guide: Creating and registering deals in Yahoo DSP

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP's documentation on deal status lifecycle, deal expiration behavior, and how to remove a deal from a Line's targeting is available in the Yahoo DSP Help Center, which requires a platform login to access.

investigate.intro: For each active Line that targets a deal, confirm the deal is still active in the Registered Deals Dashboard and has generated impressions during the current flight period.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Navigate to 'Inventory' in the left pane and open the 'Registered Deals Dashboard'. For each deal, note its current status and whether it shows any impressions during the current campaign flight period.

For any deal showing no impressions or a status other than active, identify which Lines target this deal. Navigate to each such Line's Targeting settings and confirm the deal ID is listed in the Exchanges and Deals section.
For Lines that were created by copying a prior campaign, review the copy date against the deal's flight window to assess whether the deal was already expired at the time the new Line was activated.

Note each stale deal ID, the Lines targeting it, and the deal's current status. Record whether removing the deal ID from the Line's targeting would leave the Line with no inventory targeting at all. A Line with empty Exchanges and Deals targeting will not bid.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per Line using deal targeting, whether each targeted deal is active in the Registered Deals Dashboard and shows impressions during the current flight period. A Line whose deals are all active and delivering passes. A Line that targets a deal with no impressions or an inactive status is a potential fail; the practitioner confirms the deal's status in the dashboard and records it as a confirmed fail if the deal is expired or seller-deactivated. The fail is recorded with the Line name, the stale deal ID, and the deal's current status.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each Line's targeted deal IDs alongside each deal's current status and impression count in the Registered Deals Dashboard to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more active Lines on {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP account target deal IDs that are no longer active in the Registered Deals Dashboard. The deals appear in the Lines' targeting configuration but show no impressions during the current flight period, and at least one deal has an expired or inactive status. The Lines continue to send bid requests against inventory the seller is no longer offering under those deal terms. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the Registered Deals Dashboard alongside each Line's Exchanges and Deals targeting, removing any deal IDs that are expired or inactive, and implementing a pre-launch review step for any campaign Lines created by copying from a prior flight.
